coredata.dc:title: Experimental and ensemble machine learning analyses of heat transfer, friction factor and thermal performance factor of rGO/water nanofluids in a tube

coredata.dc:creator: Sundar, L. Syam
Mewada, Hiren K.
Sangaraju, Sambasivam
coredata.dc:description: This paper explains about the experimental evaluation of thermophysical properties, heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number, friction factor, and thermal performance factor of water-based reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanofluids passes through a tube under turbulent flow conditions. The rGO nanoparticles in this study was prepared based on the Hummer's procedure. The thermophysical properties were estimated at different volume loadings from 0.5 % to 2.0 % and at different temperatures from 20 °C to 60 °C, respectively, similarly, the heat transfer and friction factor experiments were conducted in the Reynolds number ranging from 1800 to 21,000. The obtained data of heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number, friction factor and thermal performance factor was predicted based on the advanced machine learning algorithms of boosted regression tree (BRT), and extreme gradient boosting (XGB), respectively. Results indicated that, at 2.0 % vol. of rGO/water nanofluid the thermal conductivity is raised by 26.95 % at a temperature of 60 °C, viscosity is raised by 63.29 % at a temperature of 20 °C, over base fluid. Moreover, the results are further shown that, at 2.0 % vol. of nanofluid the Nusselt number, heat transfer coefficient, factor in friction, drop in pressure, and pumping power raise of 34.80 %, 44.19 %, 16.73 %, 10.48 %, and 4.55 % at a Reynolds number of 13,705, against water. The correlation coefficient (R2) results predicted from BRT and XGB models for Nusselt number data is 0.9831 and 0.994, respectively. It is understood that the XBG algorithm predicts very accurate values compared to BRT algorithm with a less mean square error. The overall performance of the system was raised by 1.28 -folds than water. Using the experimental data, new Nusselt number and friction factor correlations were proposed.

head.ce:title: Experimental and ensemble machine learning analyses of heat transfer, friction factor and thermal performance factor of rGO/water nanofluids in a tube

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper explains about the experimental evaluation of thermophysical properties, heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number, friction factor, and thermal performance factor of water-based reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanofluids passes through a tube under turbulent flow conditions. The rGO nanoparticles in this study was prepared based on the Hummer's procedure. The thermophysical properties were estimated at different volume loadings from 0.5 % to 2.0 % and at different temperatures from 20 °C to 60 °C, respectively, similarly, the heat transfer and friction factor experiments were conducted in the Reynolds number ranging from 1800 to 21,000. The obtained data of heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number, friction factor and thermal performance factor was predicted based on the advanced machine learning algorithms of boosted regression tree (BRT), and extreme gradient boosting (XGB), respectively. Results indicated that, at 2.0 % vol. of rGO/water nanofluid the thermal conductivity is raised by 26.95 % at a temperature of 60 °C, viscosity is raised by 63.29 % at a temperature of 20 °C, over base fluid. Moreover, the results are further shown that, at 2.0 % vol. of nanofluid the Nusselt number, heat transfer coefficient, factor in friction, drop in pressure, and pumping power raise of 34.80 %, 44.19 %, 16.73 %, 10.48 %, and 4.55 % at a Reynolds number of 13,705, against water. The correlation coefficient (R2) results predicted from BRT and XGB models for Nusselt number data is 0.9831 and 0.994, respectively. It is understood that the XBG algorithm predicts very accurate values compared to BRT algorithm with a less mean square error. The overall performance of the system was raised by 1.28 -folds than water. Using the experimental data, new Nusselt number and friction factor correlations were proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: A type of heat transfer fluid (HTF) with high thermal conductivity nanoparticles mixed in with the base fluid is referred to as a "nanofluid." (Choi [1]). The effectiveness of the nanofluids can be impacted by the physical and thermal characteristics of the nanoparticles, such as their size and thermal conductivity. Nanofluids have garnered a lot of attention in the last 10 years because of their significantly larger thermal conductivity over base fluids. Fluid mixed by nanoparticles may have improved thermal characteristics due to the increased thermal conductivity of the nanometer-sized particles scattered throughout the base fluid. This may also result in a rise in the rate of heat transfer, which could eventually boost the energy systems' efficiency.

Graphene is a hexagonal, two-dimensional material that is one atom thick and is created when carbon atoms are arranged in various planes. Because of its structure, graphene exhibits exceptional mechanical, thermal, and electrical capabilities, drawing the attention of numerous researchers [4]. Graphene exhibits exceptional heat transmission capabilities; at ambient temperature, the single-layer graphene nanoparticle has a thermal conductivity of 5000 W/m K and a surface area of 2630 m2/g [5]. In comparison to graphite, graphene oxide (GO) has a poorer heat conductivity upon oxidation. Meanwhile, no sedimentation happens and the used nanofluid becomes more stable due to the GO's water-like properties [6].

The hnf of GO based nanofluids circulate through various device are explained here. Esfahani et al. [7] examined hnf and drop in pressure of GO/water through copper test section under constant heat flux conditions of 7.4, 9.1 and 12.6 kW/m2 in 0.01 wt.% to 0.1 wt.%. They observed that, the Nusselt number is does not depend on the heat flux, it only depends on the nanoparticle concentration. Yang et al. [8] evaluated the hnf of graphite/water nanofluid in a tube under laminar flow conditions, and they observed, the lower hnf predicted when using the heat transfer correlation for homogeneous fluids. Bai et al. [9] observed the ratio of Nusselt number of nanofluid over Nusselt number of base fluid (Nunf /Nubf ) increases from 1.02 to 1.18 fold with an increase of volume loadings. They also observed friction coefficient decrease of 71 % at 0.1 vol.% of GO/water compared to water. Convective hnf of GO/water nanofluids in the weight concentration ranging from 0.025 wt.% to 0.1 wt.% flow in a horizontal tube was experimentally studied by Naghash et al. [10], and they observed at 0.1 wt.%, thermal conductivity enhancement of 3.8 % and heat transfer increase of 34 %, over base fluid.

Baby and Ramaprabhu [11] prepared hydrogen exfoliated graphene dispersed deionized water, and ethylene glycol based nanofluids and observed at 0.05 % vol., knf is larger about 16 %, and 75 % at a temperature of 25 °C and 50 °C, and also observed, an increase of the Nusselt number due to the increase in thermal conductivity. Ranjbarzadeh et al. [12] studied hnf , and friction factor (f) of water/graphene oxide nanofluid passes through a tube at 0 %, 0.025 %, 0.05 %, 0.075 %, and 0.1% vol. loadings, and the Reynolds number (Re) range from 5250 to 36,500, and they noticed knf raise of 28 %, friction factor increase of 16 %, and thermal performance improve of 1.148, over base fluid.

The impact of graphene oxide/ethylene glycol nanofluid on thermal conductivity was investigated by Yu et al. [13]. The researchers have noticed 86 % increase in knf at 5% vol. of nanofluid and hnf increase in the range from 4.9 to 6.9 W/mK. Both statistically and empirically, Sadeghinejad et al. [14] examined the enhancement of hnf in a circular tube with fixed heat flux condition when there is a flow of water/graphene nanofluid in the turbulent domain. They demonstrated that there was a respectable overlap between the findings of the empirical and numerical investigations. With regard to the various weight fractions, the Nusselt number indicated an improvement between 3 % and 83 %. Furthermore, they noticed raise in pressure loss about 0.4–14.6 %. An empirical investigation on the graphene oxide nanofluids thermal conductivity was conducted by Hajjar et al. [15]. At a 0.25 wt.%, the nanofluid is exhibited by 47.5 % increase in thermal conductivity. The hnf and loss in pressure of water/graphene passes in a double-pipe heat exchanger under fixed boundary conditions were examined by Sadeghinejad et al. [16], they conducted experiments under the nanofluids weight fraction ranging from 0.75 to 0.1 %, they observed greatest thermal performance coefficient of 1.77, and hnf raise of about 13–160 %, against water data.

Mehrali et al. [17] looked at the convective heat transport of water/graphene nanofluid. They stated that a nanofluid running in a horizontal pipe may have a thermal performance coefficient of 1.15. Karabulut [18] observed heat transfer increment of 48 % at 0.02 vol% of graphene oxide/water nanofluid in a tube at a mass flow rate
(
m ˙
)
of 1.5 l/min, and at a Re of 5033, against water. Babu Rao [19] prepared 50:50 % of EG/W -based graphene oxide nanofluids at 0.02 %, 0.03 %, and 0.04 % vol. loadings and conducted experiments in the mass flow rate ranging from 3 to 6 lit/min. They observed a maximum of 56.45 %, and 41.47 % improvement in effectiveness at 0.03 % vol. and at 5 lit/min flow rate and at 40 °C and 50 °C inlet temperatures, respectively. Torii [20] found an enhanced hnf performance in pipe flow using graphene-oxide nanofluids. Sajjad et al. [21] conducted heat transfer studied numerically in the Re ranging from 400 to 2000, and weight loadings ranging from 0.01 % to 0.1 %, and found hnf increase of 13.04 % at 0.1 wt.% at a Reynolds number of 2000, and also observed a maximum pressure drop ratio of 2.12 at a Re of 400 and at 0.10 wt.%.

Elghool et al. [22] explained the importance of the production of green energy through the thermo-electric generator (TEG) coupled with heat sink in their review paper. Elghool et al. [23] experimentally estimated the pumping power, and efficiency of heat pipe heat sink and observed an in improvement in thermo-electric generation and also observed power and efficiency increase of 8.2 W and 3 %, respectively. Shu et al. [24] used three-dimensional numerical model for the analysis of a thermoelectric generator (TEG) system using the engine waste heat recovery and observed 13.4 % increase of maximum output power compared with the original one. Dheyab et al. [25] analyzed the air gap of a rectangular solar air heater and found that highest air temperature difference was found for an air gap height of 3 cm is 57.5 °C. They also observed highest mass flow rate and highest heat gain of 9.0 g/s at 9 cm and 13:00 and also high efficiency of 57.3 %. Zaidan et al. [26] analyzed the heat transfer and flow characteristic of air over a flat finned tube with perforated and non-perforated fin numerically and they found Nusselt number by using the perforation technique provides 8.5 %, 13.6 % and 18.4 % enhancement using circular, square, and triangular perforation, respectively.

Ibrahim et al. [27] estimated the forced convection heat transfer of fins contain different perforated shapes of circular, rectangular, triangular and non-perforated fins and found highest temperature different of the fin with circular perforation shape is 51.29 %, rectangular perforated shape is 45.57 %, triangular perforated shape is 42.28 %, and non-perforated fin is 35.82 %. Ibrahim et al. [28] analyzed natural convection heat transfer of rectangular fins (15 fins) with a size of 100 × 270 mm embedded with circular vertical shaft, and they observed temperature distribution of non-perforated fin decreases from 49 to 42 °C, but for the perforated fin with hole diameter of 20 mm, temperature distribution decreases from 67 to 47.4 °C with low power of 200 W. They also observed under higher power of 900 W, the temperature drop of non-perforated fin decreased from 170 to 122 °C, but the temperature drop of perforated fin with hole diameter 20 mm decreased from 170 to 101.7 °C.

Pazarlioglu, and Ekiciler [29] analyzed heat transfer numerically for the flow of Al2O3/water in an elliptical dimpled tube and observed at 1.0 % Al2O3/DW nanofluid, the Nusselt number is improved by 44.56 %, and total entropy generation decrease of 37.75 % compared to the water. Ekiciler [30] studied the turbulent convective heat transfer in a confined slot and submerged impinging jet by using water and CoFe3O2/water nanofluid in the Reynolds number ranging from 6000 to 24,000 through SIMPLE algorithm and observed an enhanced heat transfer coefficient. Dehghan et al. [31] conducted heat transfer numerical study for the graphene oxide/water nanofluids inside a double backward-facing step (BFS) microchannel and found Nusselt number increase of 12.7 %, and heat transfer coefficient increase of 12.3 % at 4 % vol. and at a Reynolds numbers of 50. Farooq et al. [32] used the role of iron (III) oxide in heat transfer enhancement over the rotating disk in an axisymmetric flow and they found with an increment in nanoparticle volumetric concentration the radial velocity decline where a tangential motion of flow enhances.

The soft computing machine learning tools are now using by various researchers to predict the obtained experimental thermophysical properties and heat transfer data. These machine learning algorithms will give an accurate prediction with less time. Kanti et al. [33] estimated knf , and μ nf of 50:50 % GO/CuO–water nanofluid in the volume loadings from 0.1 % to 1.0 % and in the temperature range of 30 to 60 °C, and predicted the data by using Bayesian optimized Support Vector Machine (BoA–SVM) and Bayesian optimized Boosted Regression Trees (BoA–BRT) models, and they found BoA–SVM model predicts high accuracy data than the BoA–BRT model. Kanti et al. [34] prepared water-based Al2O3/GO nanofluids, and studied thermal conductivity, and viscosity under 0.1 % to 1 % vol. loadings, and under 30 °C to 60 °C and used a Bayesian optimized support vector machine and a wide neural network to predict the obtained data and found there is a 97.15–99.91 % accuracy of the data with those algorithms.

Kanti et al. [35] prepared water -based GO, GO–SiO2 (50:50), GO–TiO2 (50:50), and Al2O3 nanofluids and studied the hnf and hydrogen storage of LaNi5 MH reactor and observed the reduction duration by 61.7 % of water to attain 90 % hydrogen storage capacity, and they also predicted the parameters using Gene Expression Programming (GEP). Said et al. [36] considered MWCNT+Fe3O4 nanofluids in a solar collector and used boosted regression tree (BRT) and XGBoost (XGB) models. They found R2 for the BRT and XGB models predicts 0.9619, and 0.9914. They also found the mean square error for BRT and XGB models is 0.000081, and 9.11, and the MAPE is 0.0025, and 0.3114. Alizadeh et al. [37] used support machine velocity (SMV) model to examine the velocity profiles for Cu-Al2O3/water hybrid nanofluid. Sharma et al. [38] measured knf of 228 data points of TiO2/water nanofluids and used ANN, GBR, SVR, DTR, and RFR models and observed that GBR algorithm is best algorithm.

Previous studies have shown that, with the usage of nanofluids in a tube its heat transfer coefficient is enhanced. Similarly, previous studies are also shown that, with the use of different machine learning techniques, the experimental data is perfectly predicted, but mostly researchers are used machine learning technique for the properties of the nanofluids. This paper focuses on the estimation of heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number and friction factor of rGO/water nanofluid flow in a tube experimentally and then predicted with machine learning techniques which is not covered so far.

Hence the present work is undertaken to prepare the water based rGO nanofluids, and studied the thermophysical properties, heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number, friction factor, pumping power and thermal performance factor characteristics experimentally. The rGO nanosheets were prepared by using the Hummer's method [39] and characterized with XRD and FTIR. The stable water based rGO nanofluids were prepared at different volume loadings from 0.5 % to 2.0 %, and evaluated thermophysical properties in the temperature range from 20 °C to 60 °C, respectively. Moreover, the heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number, and friction factor experiments were conducted in the Reynolds number ranging from 1800 to 21,000. The experimental results were compared with those of other type of nanofluids. Two machine learning tools like Boosted Regression Tree (BRT) and Extreme gradient boosting (XGB) were used to predict the experimentally obtained heat transfer, Nusselt number, friction factor and thermal performance factor. The ANOVA analysis was also included to identify the best statistical results. Generalized Nusselt number and friction factor equations were proposed based on the experimental data.

The Hummers method was used to prepare the 2-dimensional reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanoparticles. 5 g of graphite flakes were mixed in 100 ml of H2SO4 and mixed 0.025 M of NaNO3, and then cooled with ice-water. Added 0.039 M of potassium permanganate to the above solution and mixed well for 20 min at 0 °C. The change of color of the solution was noticed due to the availability of oxidizing agent (MnO3+). The temperature was bringing back to normal by adding water. Added water to the above solution and cleaned with 70 ml of H2O2 for 30 min. Washed and centrifuged the solution several times by adding water. The prepared rGO was heated at 70 °C for 8 h. Through the same procedure, larger amount of rGO was prepared.

Fig. 1 (a) illustrates the XRD patterns of rGO. It was seen that, the highest peak of (002) stands for the rGO peak with 2θ angle of 11.68°. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) indicated in Fig. 1(b) gives the availability of groups. The CC group and CO groups were seen at 1624 cm−1 and 1725 cm−1. These groups provide an information about the –COOH. The 1045, 1222, and 1411 cm−1 peaks stands for C–O–C epoxy or alkoxy groups.

The stable rGO/water nanofluids were developed with SDBS surfactant, which was used 1/10th of weight of rGO. Eq. (1) (Alklaibi et al. [40]) estimated the rGO needed for 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 % and 2.0 % vol. loadings.

Total 10 l capacity rGO/water nanofluid was prepared by mixing 95.98, 192.93, 290.86, and 389.8 g of rGO into 10 l of water. Physical properties of rGO and water is provided in Table 1 .

With the help of the ZetaSizer Nano ZS provided by Malvern Instruments, USA, the stability of the produced rGO nanofluids was examined. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique is the basis for the instrument's operation. Twenty milliliters of each nanofluid are added to a cuvette, which is then put inside of an apparatus. Zeta potential is used to express the stability of the nanofluid. The prepared nanofluids are stable nanofluids if the zeta potential value is 30 mV or less. The surfactant gives the nanoparticles a positive or negative charge because it was added to the base fluid before the particles were mixed. There is a repulsive force between the nanoparticles if they are fully released with charge, and they eventually scatter equally into the base fluid. The results revealed that the zeta potential values for 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 % vol. loadings are, respectively, −43.6, −42.5, −41.6, and −40.3 mV. The relationship between the particles' zeta potential and volume loadings is well recognized. This indicates that the repulsive forces steadily decrease as the base fluid's volume loading increases.

For the evaluation of hnf , the prepared rGO nanofluids properties are necessary and those are determined using the instruments. The KD-2 pro equipment was utilized to assess the knf of nanofluids over 20–60 °C temperature range. Fig. 2 (a) stands the knf of prepared nanofluids. The knf riase is about 5.48 %, 8.14 %, 10.30 %, and 13.29 %, with the mixing of 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 %, vol. of nanofluids at 20 °C, whereas, the knf is further enhanced to 12.10 %, 16.69 %, 22.21 %, and 26.95 % at 60 °C, over the water data. Among the suggested techniques for raising knf are Brownian motion, nanoparticle aggregation, and molecular level liquid layering at the nanoparticles-liquid interface.

Using the A&D vibro-viscometer, viscosity of nanofluids (μ nf ) were evaluated over 20–60 °C temperature range. Fig. 2(b) stands the μ nf of rGO/water nanofluids. The μ nf raise of 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 % vol. of nanofluid is 24.30 %, 37.97 %, 43.04 %, and 63.29 % at 20 °C; moreover the μ nf is 12.50 %, 20.83 %, 33.33 %, and 41.67 %, at 60 °C, over water.

Through the Archimedes principle, the ρ nf is estimated. Fig. 2(c) gives ρ nf of nanofluids and seen that, at 2.0 %, the ρ nf is 1056.78 kg/m3 at 20 °C, and it is 1021.27 kg/m3 at 60 °C, which is higher than water. The DSC instrument was used to find the specific heat (Cp ) of nanofluids. Fig. 2(d) provides C
p,w is 4178 J/kg K at 20 °C, it is lower to 4107.3 J/kg K at 2.0 %; however, C
p,w is 4183 J/kg K at 60 °C, it is lower to 4112.2 J/kg K. Properties data is mentioned in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat transfer coefficient and friction factor studies
ce:section.ce:para: The line diagram of a setup used for the measurements were provided through Fig. 3 (a) and the picture is shown in Fig. 3(b). Copper tube of inner diameter 0.019 m, outer diameter 0.021 m, and length 1.75 m were considered. Heating coil of nichrome was wounded around the tube. The fluid temperatures (inlet, outlet), and tube surface temperatures were recorded through J-Type thermocouples. The surface temperatures were measured by keeping the J-Type thermocouples in equal distance from the left side. Thermocouple wires are coupled to data-logger, and then it is coupled to PC to record data. Through AC pump, the rGO nanofluids circulate in a tube. The raised outlet temperature is drop down to inlet temperature with Julabo water chiller for the purpose of closed circulation of nanofluids. Flow meter was used to note down the mass flow rate of the fluid.

The prepared rGO/water nanofluids were filled in a tank one after another, then switched the pump. Flow of nanofluids were take place in the tube and then switch on the heater and note down the temperature readings each 15 min interval. The difference of two set of temperatures are less means, it is considered to be the system reached the steady state. The final data was noted and used for the heat transfer evaluations. U-tube pressure tapping device is considered to evaluate the pressure drop across the test tube.

Flow rates of nanofluids in a tube was varied from 2 to 15 lit/min (0.033–0.25 kg/s), which was regulated with pump. The flow rates were converted into non-dimensional Reynolds number

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ensemble machine learning and ANOVA analysis

ce:section.ce:para: Boosted regression trees (BRT) is a powerful machine learning technique that combines the strengths of decision trees and boosting algorithms to create an ensemble model for regression tasks. This approach involves iteratively building a sequence of regression trees, where each subsequent tree is constructed to correct the errors made by the previous ones. By fitting the trees to the residuals of the previous trees, the algorithm gradually improves the predictive accuracy and handles complex relationships in the data. BRT excel in capturing non-linear patterns and handling outliers, making them well-suited for regression problems where accurate predictions are crucial. The combination of decision trees and boosting techniques allows for the modeling of intricate interactions between features and provides robust predictions.
The pseudocode for training and testing of a boosted regression tree can be summarized as follows:

Training:
1.
Initialize the target variable: y
2.
Initialize the initial prediction: Fo (x) = 0
3.
For each boosting iteration, t = 1 to T.
a.

c.
Update the ensemble prediction: Ft (x) = F

4.
The final ensemble prediction is given by: F(x) = Ft (x)

In the training phase, the algorithm iteratively builds a sequence of regression trees by fitting them to the negative gradients of the loss function with respect to the current prediction. The ensemble prediction is updated at each iteration by adding the contribution of the new tree, weighted by the learning rate. The final ensemble prediction is the sum of all individual tree predictions. During testing, the trained ensemble is used to predict the target variable for new input features
x _ t e s t
. The prediction is obtained by evaluating the final ensemble prediction function F(x) using the test data. By following this training and testing procedure, boosted regression trees can effectively learn complex relationships in the training data and provide accurate predictions for new, unseen data. The tree diagram of the BRT model is shown in Fig. 4 . The sample tree for the Nusselt number data is given in Fig. 5 .

Extreme gradient boosting (XGB) is specifically designed to optimize performance and speed in both regression and classification tasks. It builds an ensemble of weak prediction models, typically decision trees, to iteratively improve the overall predictive accuracy. It leverages the concept of gradient descent to minimize a loss function and sequentially adds new trees that focus on the remaining errors from the previous iterations. XGB incorporates several advanced features, such as regularization techniques, parallel processing, and tree pruning, to enhance model generalization and prevent overfitting. Therefore, it is robust, scalable, and provides better performance. The tree diagram of the XGB model is shown in Fig. 6 . Table 3 indicates the experimental data of Nu, hnf , f, Δ P, and PP used BRT and XGB models.

Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) is a statistical method, commonly used in all those situations where a comparison is to be made between two or more techniques. It is a vital tool of analysis for the researcher that enables him to conduct test simultaneously. In the present study, BRT, and XGB machine learning techniques were used to predict the Nusselt number, heat transfer coefficient, factor in friction, drop in pressure, and pumping power. In the ANOVA t-test method is used because only two techniques of BRT, and XGB were used for the prediction of the variables.

The t-test is described as the statistical test that examines when the two technique samples greatly differ from one another, using t-distribution which is used when the standard deviation is not known, and the sample size is small. It is a tool to analyse whether the two techniques are drawn from the same data. The test is based on t-statistic, which assumes that variable is normally distributed (symmetric bell-shaped distribution) and mean is known and population variance is calculated from the sample.

In t-test null hypothesis takes the form of Ho : μ(x) = μ(y) against alternative hypothesis H 1: μ(x) ≠ μ(y), wherein, μ(x) and μ(y) represents the population means. The degree of freedom of t-test is n 1 + n 2 − 2.

A maximum variance of ± 2.8 % was found in the thermal balance between the heat supplied to the test tube (Eq. (6)) and the heat absorbed by the fluid (Eq. (7)); nonetheless, the average value is used for thermal analysis. The Nusselt number for water (Eq. 9) is first determined and compared to theoretical data and data is shown in Fig. 8 (a), where a maximum divergence of ± 3 % is discovered.

The factor of friction for water (Eq. 13) is first determined and compared to theoretical data and data is shown in Fig. 8(b), where a maximum divergence of ± 3.2 % is discovered.

The hnf is determined using the law of conservation of energy. Although the hnf is relay on tube diameter (di ) and operational parameter
(
m ˙
)
. For this reason, the hnf is changed to Nu. Since the Nusselt number change with respect to Reynolds number is discussed in the majority of previous research, Fig. 9 (a) presents the same Nusselt number along Re and ϕ in a similar manner. As can be observed, the Nu increases as Re and ϕ grow, however under the lower Re values, the Nu increase is lower, but at higher Re values, the Nu increase is higher.

Under 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 % vol. loadings, the Nu is raised to 6.85 %, 9.07 %, 12.89 %, and 16.6 %, and at Re of 2268, 2193, 2013, and 1827, respectively, meanwhile, the Nu is larger to 19.15 %, 24.17 %, 28.35 %, and 34.80 % at Re of 17,016, 16,449, 15,100, and 13,705, over water. The larger of knf gives larger of hnf but μ nf enhance is larger of boundary layer, this turns into lesser hnf . Under the used volume loadings of nanofluids, the knf may overcome the influence of increases of μ nf , which leads to an increase in the hnf , and it leads to an increase of Nusselt number.

The Nu data for rGO/water nanofluid is validated with literature data. The change of Nu against Re and ϕ of rGO along with literature is illustrated in Fig. 9(b). Figure shows current study rGO Nu is higher than other nanofluids. In all the cases base fluid is DI-water, but Nu values are differently obtained, this is due to the use of different nanoparticles. The Nu of current study of 2.0 % vol. is higher than the other literature data, compared to Asirvatham et al. [48] data, it is 24.5 % larger; against Azmi et al. [49] data, the present data is 26 % higher; over Sundar et al. [50] data, the present data is 34 % larger; over, Duangthongsuk, and Wongwises [51] data, the present study is 36 % higher.

Abreu et al. [52] observed Nusselt number increase of 23 % at 0.25 % vol. of MWCNT/water nanofluids, when they flow in a 6 mm inner diameter and 1200 mm length pipe. Kumar et al. [53] found a maximum increase in Nusselt number for GO–water NF is 16.48 %, for rGO–CuO–water NF is 23.84 %, and 6.3 % compared to DI-water and GO-water NFs at Reynolds numbers 806.67, 1210.48, and 1617.21, respectively, when they flow in a heat exchanger. Bai et al. [54] observed Nunf /Nubf of GO-DI nanofluids increases from 1.02 to 1.18 as volume fraction rises, friction coefficient decreases of 71 % compared to deionized water, at 0.1 vol% of GO and 3 N normal load were applied.

The experimental Nusselt number of 70 data points were used for BRT and XGB models. Out of 70 data points, 56 data points are considered to train models, whereas, 14 data points are utilized to test models. From models, the R2, MSE, and MAE were analyzed. The experimental and the trained data was plotted in Fig. 9(c). As it is seen from the figure, the data points of both the models are almost perfectly match with the experimental Nusselt number, but the XGB model predicts high accuracy values, when compared to BRT model. The experimental and the tested was shown in Fig. 9(d). In this figure also, the experimental Nusselt numbed data is perfectly match with the model's data, but here also, the XGB predicts high accuracy than the BRT model.

The R2 values for BRT model under train, test, and all the results phases were 0.999, 0.89, and 0.9831, respectively. Moreover, the R2 values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.999, 0.991, and 0.994, respectively, these values are slightly higher. The RMSE values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.001, 11.64, and 5.209, respectively. However, the RMSE values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.558, 6.675, and 3.026, respectively. The MAE data of BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.001, 8.269, and 1.654, respectively. However, the MAE data for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.435, 5.29, and 1406, respectively. The lesser prediction error, perfect correlation coefficient, and lower the uncertainty impact gives XGB model is robust model compared to BRT.

Using a device that demonstrated experimental confidence, additional research was carried out on nanofluids within the Reynolds number range of 1827–20,965. Since the majority of studies have focused on hnf in relation to Re and ϕ. Fig. 10 (a) in a comparable fashion, with hnf being determined at different ϕ and Re values using Eq. (9). An increase in ϕ and Re causes hnf to rise. One of the reasons to increase the hnf is also the flow rate of nanofluid.

Assume that a fluid with a higher mass flow rate would absorb more heat and produce larger hnf values. Particle migration, enhanced knf , and Brownian motion are other factors contributing to increased hnf values. Furthermore, a number of publications noted that greater hnf can also be attributed to proper fluid turbulence under turbulent flow (Sundar et al. [55]). Heat transfer rates are increased when rGO nanoparticles are dispersed in water, greater particle loadings (ϕ) and mass flow rates
(

m ˙
, and ϕ, while lower mass flow rates result in smaller values of hnf . For 0.5% vol., the hnf is raised from 13.78 % to 26.89 % at Re increase from 2268 (

Similar fashion of an increased heat transfer coefficient of 6.04 % was observed by Akhavan-Zanjani et al. [56] with 0.02 % and at a Reynolds number of 10,850 when G/DW nanofluid flow in a heated tube. Akhavan-Zanjani et al. [57] found heat transfer coefficient increase of 14.2 % at 0.02 % vol of G-DW nanofluid flow in a circular tube of 4.2 mm inner diameter and at a Reynolds number of 1850. Sadeghinezhad et al. [14] found heat transfer coefficient higher of 83 % at 0.1 wt.% of graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) nanofluid in a 10 mm inner diameter tube under turbulent flow. Ranjbarzadeh et al. [58] was seen 40.3 % enhancement in heat transfer coefficient with a pressure loss of 16 % for the flow of GO/DW nanofluid flow in 8.5 mm inner diameter tube at 0.1 % and at a Reynolds number of 36,500.

Conduction mode of heat transfer that occurs in the fluid medium. The combination of rGO nanoparticles and water molecules in a nanofluid medium forms a homogeneous mixer. When heat is applied to nanofluids, the nanoparticles attract larger heat energy over water, which causes heat from the nanoparticles to be transferred to the water. This phenomenon results in nanofluids having a higher hnf than water. However, this idea is invalid with pure water as the entire solution is made up of water molecules. Data implies, hnf increases including the contact between solid particles and liquid particles.

Based on thermophysical characteristics of rGO nanofluids, it can be observed that two primary properties, knf and μ nf , have a greater impact on hnf . Raised ϕ values also result in higher knf and μ nf values. The fluids ability to absorb heat increases with increasing knf . Similarly, a greater μ nf corresponds to a higher flow resistance of rGO nanofluid in the tube. For nanofluids over water, the Re is reduced because of increase of μ nf at fixed mass flow rate. According to Sundar et al. [59], the nanofluids are advantageous hnf based on C μ/Ck < 4. The calculated thermophysical properties show that for all the values, C μ/Ck < 4. This indicates generated rGO are favorable hnf , meaning that knf predominates over μ nf .

The hnf values of present data at 2.0 % vol. is mentioned in Fig. 10(b) along with literature values. In all the cases, the base fluid is water, but the type of nanoparticles is different. Figure clearly shows, Asirvatham et al. [48] data is predicting higher followed by Duangthongsuk, and Wongwises [51] data in comparison with rGO/water nanofluids. But compared to Azmi et al. [49] data, present data offering larger hnf . The operating conditions, geometrical conditions, and type of nanofluids will influence on the heat transfer coefficients.

The experimental hnf of 70 data points were used for BRT and XGB models. Out of 70 data points, 56 data points are utilized to train models, whereas, 14 data points are utilized to test models. From models, the R2, MSE, and MAE were analyzed. The experimental and the trained data was plotted in Fig. 10(c). As it is seen from the figure, the data points of both the models are almost perfectly match with the experimental hnf , but the XGB model predicts high accuracy values, when compared to BRT model. The experimental and the tested was shown in Fig. 10(d). In this figure also, the experimental hnf data is perfectly match with the model's data, but here also, the XGB.

The R2 values for BRT model for train, test, and all the results phases were 0.999, 0.907, and 0.985, respectively. Moreover, the R2 values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.999, 0.9598, and 0.993, respectively, these values are slightly higher. The RMSE values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.066, 402.83, and 180.15, respectively. However, the RMSE values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 26.947, 264.71, and 120.81, respectively. The MAE data of BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.048, 298.19, and 59.67, respectively. However, the MAE data for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 22.457, 216.676, and 61.301, respectively. The lesser prediction error, perfect correlation coefficient, and lower the uncertainty impact gives XGB model is robust model compared to BRT.

Obtained experimental f data is given in Fig. 11 (a) along Re and ϕ. The f goes higher to 5.94 %, 8.24 %, 9.62 %, and 12.15 %, at ϕ = 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0% vol. of nanofluids in a tube under Re of 2268, 2193, 2013, and 1827; the f higher to 9.53 %, 13.33 %, 14.09 %, and 16.76 % were noticed at ϕ = 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 %, vol. of nanofluids in a tube at Re of 17,016, 16,449, 15,100, and 13,705, respectively, over water.

The friction factor of rGO/water nanofluids is validated with literature data in Fig. 11(b). Figure indicates, Sundar et al. [50] data, Azmi et al. [49] data, shows higher values compared to rGO nanofluids. Similarly, the Duangthongsuk, and Wongwises [51] data is lower than rGO data.

Experimental f of 70 data points were used for BRT and XGB models. Out of 70 data points, 56 data points are utilized to train models, whereas, 14 data points are utilized to test models. From models, the R2, MSE, and MAE were analyzed. The experimental and the trained data was plotted in Fig. 9(c). As it is seen from the figure, the data points of both the models are almost perfectly match with the experimental f, but the XGB model predicts high accuracy values, when compared to BRT model. The experimental and the tested was shown in Fig. 11(d). In this figure also, the experimental f data is perfectly match with the model's data, but here also, the XGB.

The R2 values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.9969, 0.9667, and 0.9927, respectively. Moreover, the R2 values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.9999, 0.9816, and 0.9973, respectively, these values are slightly higher. The RMSE values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0003, 0.0008, and 0.0004, respectively. However, the RMSE values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 5.073, 0.0003, and 0.0002, respectively. The MAE data of BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0001, 0.0006, and 0.0002, respectively. However, the MAE data for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 3.857, 0.0004, and 0.0001, respectively. The lesser prediction error, perfect correlation coefficient, and lower the uncertainty impact gives XGB model is robust model compared to BRT.

It noted that, the rGO nanofluids passes a tube under fixed rates. In most of the industrial heat exchangers, the fluid flow rates are fixed, but by mixing particles in base fluids gives higher μ values. By utilizing these nanofluids through a component pump decreases the
m ˙
rates. The necessary thing is to analyze the power required to pump the fluid in a tube. The U-tube manometer was used to analyze the pressure drop. Drop-in pressure of nanofluids under Re and ϕ are illustrated in Fig. 12 (a). The Re of 2268, 2193, 2013, and 1827, the drop-in pressure is raised about 4.66 %, 6.28 %, 7.75 %, and 11.33 %, at 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 %; moreover, at a Re of 17,016, 16,449, 15,100, and 13,705, the pressure drop is augmented by 8.20 %, 11.28 %, 12.14 %, and 15.90 % at 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 %, compared to the water alone.

The experimental Δ p of 70 data points were used for BRT and XGB models. Out of 70 data points, 56 data points are utilized to train models, whereas, 14 data points are utilized to test models. From models, the R2, MSE, and MAE were analyzed. The experimental and the trained data was plotted in Fig. 12(b). As it is seen from the figure, the data points of both the models are almost perfectly match with the experimental Δ p, but the XGB model predicts high accuracy values, when compared to BRT model. The experimental and the tested was shown in Fig. 12(c). In this figure also, the experimental Δ p data is perfectly match with the model's data, but here also, the XGB.

The R2 values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.999, 0.822, and 0.971, respectively. Moreover, the R2 values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.999, 0.9816, and 0.992, respectively, these values are slightly higher. The RMSE values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0006, 117.71, and 52.64, respectively. However, the RMSE values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 5.073, 65.9, and 29.85, respectively. The MAE data of BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0052, 86.81, and 17.36, respectively. However, the MAE data for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 3.8576, 47.22, and 12.63, respectively. The lesser prediction error, perfect correlation coefficient, and lower the uncertainty impact gives XGB model is robust model compared to BRT.

Eq. (13) is considered the pumping power evaluations. Fig. 13 (a) demonstrates the evaluated pumping power. The pumping power is higher about 3.38 %, 4.36 %, 5.8 %, and 5.35 %, while using ϕ = 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 % nanofluids in a tube under Re of 2268, 2193, 2013, and 1827; it higher about 6.88 %, 9.26 %, 9.37 %, and 9.67 % at ϕ = 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 %, vol. of nanofluids in a tube at Re of 17,016, 16,449, 15,100, and 13,705, respectively, over water.

For both water and nanofluids, a total of 70 data points was considered for pumping power. For both BRT and XGB models 70 data points were used. Out of 70 data points, 56 data points were considered for train the models and 14 data points were used to test the models. In all the cases, the MSE, MAPE and R2 was analyzed. The output results of the training data were provided in Fig. 13(b) along with the experimental data. As it was seen from the figure, the data points of both the models are almost perfectly match with the experimental pumping power. The XGB model predicts very high accuracy when compared to the BRT model. The testing data was plotted in Fig. 13(c). In this case also, both the model's pumping power is perfectly match with the experimental pumping power. The XGB predicts high accuracy than the BRT model.

The R2 values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.9999, 0.8096, and 0.9625, respectively. Moreover, the R2 values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.999, 0.866, and 0.9787, respectively, these values are slightly higher. The RMSE values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0005, 10.0218, and 0.0048, respectively. However, the RMSE values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.001, 0.0254, and 0.0114, respectively. The MAE data of BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0005, 0.0218, and 0.0048, respectively. However, the MAE data for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0009, 0.0156, and 0.0038, respectively. The lesser prediction error, perfect correlation coefficient, and lower the uncertainty impact gives XGB model is robust model compared to BRT.

For both water and nanofluids, a total of 70 data points was considered for TPF. For both BRT and XGB models 70 data points were used. Out of 70 data points, 56 data points were considered for train the models and 14 data points were used to test the models. In all the cases, the MSE, MAPE and R2 was analyzed. The R2 values for BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.9998, 0.9093, and 0.9792, respectively. Moreover, the R2 values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.9997, 0.9448, and 0.9872, respectively, these values are slightly higher. However, the RMSE values for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0008, 0.0127, and 0.0059, respectively. The MAE data of BRT model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0005, 0.01269, and 0.00315, respectively. However, the MAE data for XGB model during train, test, and all the results phases were 0.0006, 0.0095, and 0.00255, respectively. The lesser prediction error, perfect correlation coefficient, and lower the uncertainty impact gives XGB model is robust model compared to BRT. The TPF data used for the BRT and XGB models are shown in Table 4 . The MAE, RMSE and R2 values for the properties are shown in Table 5 :

t-test, a special type of ANOVA, is used in this paper to compare two algorithms. Since the paper tests two regression models for six parameters using R2 analysis, a paired t-test is used for hypothesis testing comparing the performance of the two regression models. A t-test is performed on between the two models configuring a significance level of 0.05. The null hypothesis assumes no significant difference in the mean residuals between the models, while the alternative hypothesis suggests a significant difference. The t-test provides a p-value, which can be used to determine the statistical significance of the difference. The following table shows that the P-value for R2 analysis is less than 0.05 for BRT, and XGB, it suggests that there is strong evidence to reject the null hypothesis. This implies that one model is performing better than another model. The R2 data provided in the Table 5 is for the ANOVA analysis. The comparison of the paired t-test analysis is shown in Table 6 .

Current data of Nu and friction factor was used to develop the regression equations valid in the Reynolds number range from 1827 to 20,965, Prandtl number range from 5.71 to 7.82, volume loadings from 0 to 2.0 %.

The data obtained from the Eq. (21) is shown in Fig. 15 (a) and the Eq. (22) is shown in Fig. 15(b) along with experimental data.

The goal of the current work is to determine the thermal performance factor, pumping power, Nusselt number, heat transfer coefficient, and turbulent flow conditions of a rGO/water nanofluid flow in a tube at various Reynolds numbers and volume loadings. The findings showed that the Nusselt number and heat transfer coefficient increased with increasing particle loadings and Reynolds numbers. The second law of thermodynamics and the exergy efficiency of rGO/water nanofluids flowing in a tube with turbulence promoters such as twisted tape, longitudinal strips, and helical coil inserts may be studied in more detail under both laminar and turbulent flow conditions. Future research may also examine the impact of other base fluids on the rGO nanofluids' heat transfer coefficient, friction factor, energy efficiency, and thermal performance factor.

The experiments have been conducted to analyze the thermophysical properties, heat transfer coefficient, Nusselt number, friction factor, and thermal performance factor of water-based reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanofluids flowing in a tube at turbulent flow conditions. The data can also be used for machine learning analysis. ANOVA also performed for the data. With an increased volume loadings and flow rates, the Nusselt number is enhanced. At the same time, the thermal performance factor of the system is improved by using the nanofluids. Among the two machine learning approaches, XGB is predicting high accurate values, even ANOVA t-test results also shown the same thing. The specific conclusions are drawn here. Form different equipment's the flow properties were analyzed. At 2.0 % vol. of nanofluids, the knf is raised about 13.29 % at 20 °C, whereas, the knf is further raised about 26.95 % at 60 °C, over the water data. At 2.0 % vol. of nanofluid, the μ nf is larger by 63.29 % at 20 °C; moreover μ nf is raised about 41.67 % at 60 °C over water.

Under 2.0 % vol. loading, the Nu is raised about 16.6 % and 34.8 % and at a Re of 1827 and 13,705, over water. For 2.0 % vol. loading, the hnf is raised from 37.21 % to 58.66 % at Re increase from 1827 (
m ˙
= 0.033 kg/s) to 13,705 (
m ˙
= 0.25 kg/s) over the base fluid. The f is raised by 12.15 % and 16.76 % at ϕ = 2.0 % vol. of nanofluids in a tube at a Re of 1827 and 13,705 over water. Pressure drop is incremented by 11.33 % and 15.9 % at 2.0 % and at Re of 1827 and 13,705; pumping power is also incremented by 5.35 % and 9.67 % at 2.0 % vol. of nanofluid and at Re of 1827 and 13,705, over water. With the utilization of nanofluids, the Nu and friction factor is enhanced. But enhanced factor in friction is drawback of the system. Hence the TPF is evaluated and noticed to be 1.28 -times at 2.0 % and at Re of 13,705, over water.

The ensembled machine learning models of BRT and XGB were used to evaluate the obtained experimental parameters. The R2 values of Nusselt number from BRT and XGB models for all the data is 0.9831, and 0.994, at the same time, the RMSE from BRT and XGB models for all the data is 5.209, and 3.026. The hnf correlation coefficient (R2) from BRT and XGB models for all the data is 0.985, and 0.993, moreover, the RMSE from BRT and XGB models for all the data is 180.15, and 120.81. The friction factor R2 data for BRT and XGB for all the data is 0.9927, and 0.9973, however, the RMSE is 0.0004, and 0.0001. The R2 and RMSE values of pressure drop from BRT and XGB models is 0.971, and 0.992, and 52.64, and 12.63. The pumping power R2 and RMSE value for BRT and XGB for all the data is 0.9625, and 0.9787, and 0.015, and 0.0114. The XGB model predicts higher values than the BRT model. The overall, thermal performance factor R2 from BRT and XGB is 0.9792, and 0.9997, with a RMSE error of 0.00315, and 0.00255, respectively. ANOVA t-test statistical analysis gives XGB results are very accurate to the experimental data compared to BRT results.

L. Syam Sundar: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hiren K. Mewada: Software, Validation, Investigation. Sambasivam Sangaraju: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology.
